volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Epidicus (Section 2.1.177b - Section 2.2.254)

sections.label: Section 2.1.177b
sections.translation: Ah me ! I was a Hercules while she was with me ; and , upon my faith , the sixth labour [*] was not more difficult to Hercules than the one that fell to my lot .

sections.label: Section 2.1.180
sections.translation: I ’ faith , money ’ s a handsome dowry .

sections.label: Section 2.1.180b
sections.translation: Troth , so it is , which isn ’ t encumbered with a wife .

sections.label: Section 2.2.181
sections.translation: Hist ! hist ! be silent , and have good courage ; with a fair omen have I come out of doors , the bird upon the left hand [*] . ( Pointing to his head .) I ’ ve got a sharp knife , with which to embowel the old man ’ s purse ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.184_185
sections.original: sed eccum ípsum ante aedis conspicor Apoecide †
sections.translation: but see ! here he is before the house of Apaecides , the two old fellows , just as I want . Now I shall change me into a leech , and suck out the blood of these who are called the pillars of the Senate .

sections.label: Section 2.2.189_190
sections.translation: Let him be married at once .

sections.label: Section 2.2.189_190b
sections.translation: I approve of your design .

sections.label: Section 2.2.191
sections.original: nam égo illum audivi in amorem haerere apud néscio quam fidicinam ,
sections.translation: For I ’ ve heard that he ’ s entangled with love with a certain music-girl , I don ’ t know who . At that I ’ m vexed to death .

sections.label: Section 2.2.192b
sections.original: Di hercle omnis me ádiuvant augent amant :
sections.translation: By my troth , all the Deities do aid , amplify , and love me ; really , these men themselves are pointing out to me the way by means of which I ’ m to get the money out of them . Now then , come , equip yourself , Epidicus , and throw your cloak about your neck ( suiting the action to the word .)

sections.label: Section 2.2.195
sections.original: itaque ádsimulato quasi per urbem totam hominem quaesiveris .
sections.translation: and pretend as though you had been in search of the man all the city over . On with it , if you are going to do it ! ( He hurries past the OLD MEN as though he didn ’ t see them , and calls out aloud .) Immortal Gods ! I do wish I could meet with Periphanes at home , whom I ’ m tired with searching for all over the city , throughout the doctors ’ shops , throughout the barbers ’ shops , in the gymnasium , and in the Forum , at the perfumers ’ shops and the butchers ’ stalls [*] , and round about the bankers ’ shops .

sections.label: Section 2.2.200
sections.original: rogitando sum raucus factus , paene in cursu concidi .
sections.translation: I ’ m become hoarse with enquiring ; I ’ ve almost dropped down with running .

sections.label: Section 2.2.201
sections.translation: Epidicus !

sections.label: Section 2.2.201b
sections.translation: Who is it that ’ s calling Epidicus back ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.201c
sections.translation: It ’ s I , Periphanes .

sections.label: Section 2.2.202
sections.translation: And I , Apaecides .

sections.label: Section 2.2.202b
sections.original: Et ego quidem sum Epidicus . sed , ere , optuma
sections.translation: And I , indeed , am Epidicus . But , master , I find that you ’ ve both met me at the nick of time .

sections.label: Section 2.2.203b
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.204
sections.translation: Wait , wait ! ( puffs and blows .) prithee , do let me get breath !

sections.label: Section 2.2.204b
sections.translation: By all means , rest yourself .

sections.label: Section 2.2.204c
sections.translation: I ’ m quite faint ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.205
sections.translation: I must recover my breath .

sections.label: Section 2.2.205b
sections.translation: Do rest yourself at your leisure .

sections.label: Section 2.2.205c
sections.translation: Lend me your attention . All the men of the army have been remanded home from Thebes [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.2.206b
sections.translation: Who knows for certain that this has been done ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.207b
sections.translation: I say that it has been done .

sections.label: Section 2.2.207c
sections.translation: Are you sure of that ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.207d
sections.translation: I am sure of it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.208
sections.translation: Why are you sure of it ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.208b
sections.original: Quia ego ire vidi milites plenis viis ;
sections.translation: Because I ’ ve seen the soldiers marching through the streets in shoals . They are bringing back their arms and their baggage-horses .

sections.label: Section 2.2.209b
sections.translation: Very good indeed !

sections.label: Section 2.2.210
sections.original: Tum captivorum quid ducunt secum ! pueros , virgines ,
sections.translation: Then , what prisoners they ’ ve got with them ! boys , girls , in twos and threes ; another one has got five ; there ’ s a crowd in the streets ; they are looking out each for his son .

sections.label: Section 2.2.212b
sections.translation: I ’ troth , a business very well managed !

sections.label: Section 2.2.213
sections.original: Tum meretricum numerus tantus , quantum in urbe omni fuit ,
sections.translation: Then , filly as many of the courtesans as there are in the whole city were going decked out each to meet her lover ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.215
sections.original: eos captabant . id adeo qui maxime animum advorterim ?
sections.translation: they were going to trap them ; that ’ s the fact , inasmuch as I gave especial attention to it ; several of these had with them nets beneath their garments . When I came to the harbour , forthwith I espied her waiting there , and with her were four music-girls .

sections.label: Section 2.2.218b
sections.translation: With whom , Epidicus ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.219
sections.original: Cum illa quam tuos gnatus annos multos deamat , deperit ,
sections.translation: With her whom your son has been loving and doting on for years ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.220
sections.original: ubi fidemque remque seque teque properat perdere ;
sections.translation: with whom he ’ s making all haste to ruin credit , property , himself , and yourself . She was on the lookout for him at the harbour .

sections.label: Section 2.2.221b
sections.translation: Just see the sorceress now !

sections.label: Section 2.2.222
sections.original: Sed vestita , aurata , ornata ut lepide , ut concinne , ut nove .
sections.translation: But decked out , sparkling with gold , and adorned so splendidly ! so nicely ! so fashionably !

sections.label: Section 2.2.223
sections.original: Quid erat induta ? an regillam induculam an mendiculam ?
sections.translation: What was she drest in ? Was it a royal robe , or was it a plain dress ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.224
sections.original: Inpluviatam , ut istaec faciunt vestimentis nomina .
sections.translation: A skylight one [*] , according as these women coin names for garments .

sections.label: Section 2.2.225
sections.translation: What ! was she dressed in a skylight ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.225b
sections.translation: What ’ s there wonderful in that ? As though many women didn ’ t go through the streets decked out with farms upon them . But when the tax is demanded , they declare it cannot be paid [*] ; while to these hussies , to whom a larger tax is paid , it can be paid . Why , what new names every year these women are finding for their clothing —

sections.label: Section 2.2.230
sections.original: tunicam rallam , tunicam spissam , linteolum caesicium ,
sections.translation: the thin tunic , the thick tunic , your fulled linen cloth , chemises , bordered shifts ’ the marigold or saffron-coloured dress , the under-petticoat or else the light vermilion dress , the hood , the royal or the foreign robe , the wave pattern [*] or the feather-pattern , the wax or the apple-tint . The greatest nonsense ! From dogs , too , do they even take the names .

sections.label: Section 2.2.234b
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Line 234c
sections.translation: They call one the Laconian [*] . These names compel men to make auctions .

sections.label: Section 2.2.236
sections.translation: But do you say on as you commenced .

sections.label: Section 2.2.236b
sections.translation: Two other women behind me began to speak thus between themselves ; I , like my wont , went away a little distance from them ; I pretended that I wasn ’ t attending to their talk : I didn ’ t quite hear all , and still I wasn ’ t deceived

sections.label: Section 2.2.240
sections.translation: in a word they said .

sections.label: Section 2.2.240b
sections.translation: I long to hear it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.240c
sections.translation: Then one of them said to the other with whom she was talking —

sections.label: Section 2.2.241b
sections.translation: What ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.241c
sections.translation: Be quiet then , that you may hear . After they had caught sight of her whom your son is dying for : Prithee , how happily and luckily has it befallen that woman for her lover to be wishing to set her free . Who is he ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.245
sections.original: inquit altera illi . ibi illa nominat Stratippoclem
sections.translation: said the other . She mentioned Stratippocles .

sections.label: Section 2.2.246b
sections.original: Perii hercle . quid ego ex te audio ?
sections.translation: Troth now , I ’ m undone ; what is it I hear of you ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.247
sections.original: Hoc quod actum est . egomet postquam id illas audivi loqui ,
sections.translation: That which really took place . After this , I myself , when I heard them talking , began again to draw closer towards them little by little , as though the crowd of people was pushing me , whether I would or no .

sections.label: Section 2.2.249b
sections.translation: I understand .

sections.label: Section 2.2.250
sections.original: Ibi illa interrogavit illam : qui scis ? quis id dixit tibi ?
sections.translation: Then the one asked the other , How do you know ? Why , because a letter has been brought her to-day from Stratippocles ; that he has borrowed money on interest from a banker at Thebes ; that it is ready , and he himself has brought it for that purpose .

sections.label: Section 2.2.253b
sections.translation: Tell on — I ’ m undone !

sections.label: Section 2.2.254
sections.original: Haec sic aibat : sic audivisse ex eapse atque epistula .
sections.translation: She said that she had heard so from her and from the letter which she had seen .
